data.title: Cornyn shares fable of frog and scorpion after Trump sinks re-election
data.description:
Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) cryptically shared the fable of the frog and the scorpion on social media after President Trump torpedoed his re-election campaign by endorsing state Attorney General Ken Paxton, who cruised to victory in the Texas Senate GOP primary Tuesday.

Cornyn, who touted his record of voting with Trump 99.3 percent of the time, posted the famous fable about betrayal on the social media platform X, but left it ambiguous what the coded message was intended to say.

“An old but apt fable,” Cornyn wrote on Friday morning above the story of a scorpion who wants to cross a river but cannot swim.

Cornyn recounts how the scorpion asks the frog for a ride to the other side but the frog hesitates, worried that it might get stung.

The scorpion points out to the reluctant frog that it would also drown if it stung and killed the frog in the middle of the river, convincing it to agree to the proposition.

“Midway across the river, the scorpion stings the frog anyway, dooming them both. The dying frog asks the scorpion why it stung despite knowing the consequence, to which the scorpion replies: ‘I am sorry, but I couldn’t help myself. It’s my character,’” Cornyn posted.

The post didn’t include any additional commentary and Cornyn’s office didn’t comment on what the senator was trying to convey by sharing the parable.

But the story raises questions about what Cornyn might be thinking about Trump’s decision to endorse Paxton, whom Senate Republican leaders viewed as a much weaker candidate than Cornyn to defend the Texas Senate seat in the general election.

Trump on Sunday blasted Cornyn on Truth Social as “VERY disloyal to me” because he “didn’t fight hard enough for the desperately needed SAVE AMERICA ACT.”

That sharp criticism might not be sitting well with Cornyn who flipped his position on preserving the Senate filibuster in March to call in a New York Post-published op-ed on Senate Republicans to change the rules to pass the SAVE America Act.

“After careful consideration, I support whatever changes to Senate rules that may prove necessary for us to get the SAVE America Act and homeland security funding past the Democrats’ obstruction, through the Senate, and on the president’s desk for his signature,” Cornyn wrote in The Post, breaking from the pledge he made during the 2024 Senate GOP leadership race to preserve the 60-vote threshold for passing legislation if elected Senate Majority Leader.

Cornyn also served as Senate Republican whip during the first two years of Trump’s first term in the White House and played a key role in helping to pass the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, one of Trump’s signature legislative achievements

Cornyn noted that he had a stronger pro-Trump voting record than 95 out of 100 senators during Trump’s first term and that he endorsed Trump in 2024 with his earliest-ever endorsement in a contested GOP presidential primary.

That still wasn’t enough to earn Trump’s endorsement in the Texas Senate primary, even though Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and other Senate Republicans lobbied Trump for months to back Cornyn.

Senate Republican strategists had circulated an estimate before Tuesday’s primary that it would cost the party more than $200 million to help Paxton beat Democratic candidate James Talarico in the general election.

data.title: Most artists announced for 'Great American State Fair' have backed out. Who is still playing?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – More than half of the musical acts that were announced to perform during the “Great American State Fair” have already pulled out of the concert series, with some citing concerns over what they believe to be a partisan or politically charged event.

Freedom 250, a President Donald Trump-backed organization tasked with helping to plan semiquincentennial celebrations, had only this week announced the performers as part of the “first wave” of entertainers and speakers appearing at its Great American State Fair. The fair, which will take place on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., is being held between June 25 and July 10.

“The Great American State Fair will feature mainstage performances by legendary artists every Thursday, Friday, and Saturday night,” Freedom 250 announced in a Wednesday press release.

Who dropped out of the event?

In that same press release, Freedom 250 announced performances by Martina McBride, C+C Music Factory, Vanilla Ice, Milli Vanilli, Young MC, The Commodores, Morris Day and The Time, Flo Rida and Bret Michaels. But as soon as that evening, multiple entertainers said they would not be taking part.

“Contrary To Rumor, Morris Day & The Time Will Not Be Performing At The ‘GREAT AMERICAN STATE FAIR,’” a message posted to the group’s official Facebook page announced.

“Bust a Move” rapper Young MC also said Wednesday that he told his agents that he would not perform, claiming the artists were “never told about and political involvement with the event.”

“I hope to perform in D.C. in the near future at an event that is not so politically charged,” he wrote on Facebook.

The following day, The Commodores, Martina McBride and Bret Michaels announced they had dropped out, as well. Michaels, the frontman of the rock band Poison, said the event “evolved into something much more divisive than what I agreed to be a part of” and claimed he was already receiving “threats” over his participation.

Who might still perform?

Performers affiliated with two of the acts — C+C Music Factory and Milli Vanilli — have said they were surprised to learn of the groups’ involvement in the fair. In response, Freedom Williams, who appeared on some of C+C Music Factory’s biggest songs, said he told his agent to remove him from the show in an expletive-filled video he shared to social media. And a group of singers who had recorded as part of Milli Vanilli clarified that they were not performing in the show, per a statement obtained by Consequence of Sound.

“Others using the name ‘Milli Vanilli’ that appear on the advertisement should be considered a tribute band with no association vocally or musically to our sound or songs,” reads part of a statement attributed to “original” Milli Vanilli singers Jodie Rocco, Linda Rocco, Brad Howell, John Davis and Charles Shaw.

Fab Morvan, one of Milli Vanilli’s frontmen, later confirmed he would still perform at the Great American State Fair under the Milli Vanilli name, saying in a statement obtained by the Associated Press that he aims to “entertain and unite people, not divide them.”

But it remained murky if any iteration of C+C Music Factory would still be performing. Robert Civiles, a co-founder of the group, had claimed that Williams had misrepresented himself as being part of the group, and was only a “guest” who appeared on C+C Music Factory’s songs. (Civiles claimed that Williams is only allowed to bill himself as “Freedom Williams, formerly a guest featured artist on C&C Music Factory.”) But Civiles said he was not “involved in” the Great American State Fair event, and did not “endorse” it.

“We stand for love of all people and races globally and practice neutrality when it comes to political views,” he wrote on Facebook.

As of Friday morning, rappers Flo Rida and Vanilla Ice were still slated to appear at the Freedom 250 event. Vanilla Ice, in a TikTok post shared before several fellow performers canceled, announced that he was proud to participate.

“I’m super honored to do this concert with everybody. We’re gonna bring back the ‘90s,” the artist, whose real name is Rob Van Winkle, said.

Vanilla Ice also shared a link to the video on his X account, where part of his bio describes him as “NON-Political.”

When asked about current plans for entertainment, a representative for Freedom 250 said the organization’s officials “respect the rights of any artist to make their own decisions, and our doors remain open to any performer who wants to be part of honoring 250 years of American freedom, culture, and unity.”

“There is far too much to celebrate about this great nation to let noise and division distract from the incredible moment ahead — and we look forward to welcoming millions of Americans to a fair that belongs to all Americans,” Rachel Reisner, a spokesperson for Freedom 250, said in a statement obtained by Nexstar.

Reisner did not provide a timeline for the next “wave” of performers to be announced.

data.title: We all lose when journalism is biased
data.description:
When I began my career in journalism, I was based in Europe. Traveling around the continent, I learned how different the European model of journalism was from my native U.S.

In Spain, for example, El Pais catered to liberals and El Mundo to conservatives. You might be saying, well, it’s similar in the U.S. — The New York Times and The Wall Street Journal cater to similar crowds respectively. 

The difference is that partisanship in U.S. newspapers was limited to the Opinion pages. In those Spanish newspapers, editorial choices and assignments were considered with politics in mind. But in American newsrooms, a policy of dispassionate journalism above the political fray was practiced. It wasn’t always perfect, but it was the goal to strive for.  

Sadly, something has shifted within American journalism in this era of divisiveness and hyper-partisanship. The New York Times — considered by many the paragon of American journalism and one of the most important newspapers in the world — has surrendered this hallowed tradition. It is an example of the degradation of American journalism and of where the profession seems to be headed. 

The latest blow to the reputation of The Times is an odious column by Nicholas Kristoff dated May 11, in which he made the extraordinary claim that Israelis are training dogs to rape Palestinian prisoners. The absurd claims were immediately debunked by animal experts, and the Israeli government is suing The Times for defamation.  

The Times is well-known for its fact-checking rigor. But for some reason it seems to have abandoned its standards to give a star columnist his latest opportunity to slander Israel. The column relies on the word of unnamed sources and misrepresented quotes, as well as a European non-profit with well-documented ties to Hamas.  

Many critics point out that Kristoff’s column landed a day before the release of a comprehensive report, several years in the making, which painstakingly laid out the sexual atrocities inflicted by Hamas upon Israelis on Oct. 7, 2023. If you have doubts about the timing of the Kristoff column, you might be credulous enough to apply for a job as a fact-checker at The Times.

The Kristoff column comes as the latest example of The Times debasing itself and its standards when it comes specifically to the Israeli Palestinian conflict. Early in the Gaza war there was the now infamous front-page publication of a report that blamed an Israeli missile for destroying a hospital and killing hundreds. When U.S. intelligence indicated that the damage was caused by the Palestinian militant Islamic Jihad group, The Times issued a small correction buried in the paper and on social media.  

Then there was the front-page photo of a skeletal child, which was used as evidence to support the false charge that Israel was starving Gaza. It turns out that the child had cerebral palsy, which, again, The Times standard factchecking should have revealed before the story went viral, and which again resulted in a correction which far fewer saw. 

As a shocking post-script to this particular story, the photographer won the Pulitzer Prize for the photo, even though it was published under false pretenses. The prize committee, aware of the now deceitful past of the photo, nonetheless claimed that it showed “the devastation and starvation in Gaza resulting from the war with Israel” even though the charge of forced starvation faded from the media because of a lack of evidence. While The Times deserves the opprobrium heaped upon it for these lies, the Pulitzer committee provided further evidence of how deep the rot within journalism has grown.  

The erosion of objectivity at The Times comes in an era of journalistic decline generally, in part due to economic factors. The internet and social media have for decades now cut deeply into the readership of newspapers. This has led to a reduction in jobs and the number of high-quality people in the field.  

These same trends have crushed local newspapers and the crisis that has unleashed will play out over a longer timeline. These smaller newspapers have been an essential watchdog against corruption and played an important role in keeping communities informed about the good and bad unfolding around them.  

As American journalism has declined so has its professional standards. While “citizen journalists” seemed like a fun idea when social media was dawning, the lack of professionalism has contributed to a world where it’s hard to know if we can trust what we’re reading. 

Do you want to get your news from so-called “journalist” Ashley Rojas? She is covering the trial of accused assassin Luigi Mangione. She thought it would be a good idea to proudly stand outside the courtroom and say of Mangione’s alleged victim, “F— Brian Thompson. His children are better off without him.”

The reduction of trusted voices reporting the news honestly and objectively is exacerbating cultural tensions that are reaching a boiling point. The New York Times has failed its readers and abandoned a sacred tradition of the Fourth Estate, and we’re all poorer for it.  

Jeremy Hurewitz is the author of “Sell Like a Spy” and the head of Interfor Academy.

data.title: Trump approval rating slips to record low of 34 percent in YouGot/Economist poll
data.description:
President Trump’s job approval rating fell to a record low of 34 percent in this week’s YouGov/The Economist poll, marking the lowest level recorded in the survey across both of his terms in office.

The rating is also lower than any approval rating former President Biden received during his presidency, according to YouGov. The Economist noted that Trump “this week became the most unpopular president since our poll started in 2009.”

With 59 percent saying they disapprove of Trump this week, the president’s net approval rating stands at negative 26 percentage points — another record low across both of his terms and Biden’s presidency.

Trump’s approval rating is slipping across the political spectrum.

Among Republicans, his net approval stands at 61 percentage points, down 30 points since January 2025 and the lowest level of his second term, though still above the low of 53 percentage points recorded during his first term in late 2017.

Democrats and independents view Trump less favorably than Republicans, and Trump has set new records across his two terms for lowest net approval with those groups.

Among Democrats, Trump’s net approval stands at negative 96 percentage points, and, among independents, Trump’s net approval is negative 44 percentage points.

The president’s declining job approval numbers come amid heightened concerns about the economy and a prolonged war in Iran.

For the second week in a row, approximately 63 percent of respondents say the economy is getting worse, compared to just 13 percent who say it’s getting better. Since 2017, the only time that level was higher was in 2022, as inflation was soaring.

Views on the economy have gotten significantly worse since Trump took office in 2025 — an election victory many pollsters have attributed to his perceived strength on the economy, among other factors. Today, 43 percent of Americans say they and their families are worse off financially than a year ago, the highest level since 2024. And only 10 percent say they’re better off than they were a year ago.

The survey was conducted on May 22-26 and included 1,520 respondents. The margin of error was 3.6 percentage points.

data.title: Putin adviser warns EU after drone hits Romania: 'The peaceful sleep is over'
data.description:
Dmitry Medvedev, deputy chair of the Security Council of Russia and the country’s former president, cautioned the European Union on Friday to be “vigilant” amid the “war with Russia” that Europe has entered, after an errant Russian drone crashed in Romania.

“Citizens of EU countries, You should realize your authorities have unilaterally entered into a war with Russia,” the senior Russian official wrote in a post on social platform X. “So be vigilant and don’t be surprised by anything. The peaceful sleep is over. But you know who to ask why.”

The warning from Medvedev, a close ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin, came after Romanian authorities said a Russian drone that was part of an overnight attack on Ukraine crashed into an apartment building in eastern Romania, injuring two people.

NATO condemned the incident in its member country as reckless and reaffirmed its commitment to defend its alliance territory.

“We will continue to enhance our readiness to deter and defend against any threat, including from drones,” NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte wrote on X after speaking with Romanian President Nicușor Dan. “Russia’s reckless behaviour is a danger to us all. They continue to target civilians and civilian infrastructure across Ukraine. And last night showed yet again that the implications of their illegal war of aggression don’t stop at the border.”

Medvedev previously threatened EU diplomats in Ukraine’s capital, Kyiv, and the Russian Foreign Ministry demanded that foreign nationals leave Ukraine in the wake of Russia’s warnings of a large-scale attack on the city.

“The EU has said it will maintain its diplomatic presence in Kiev unchanged, despite Russia’s warnings. Well, apparently they’ve got diplomats to spare and need to trim the headcount,” Medvedev wrote Tuesday on X.

Russia has launched increasingly intense attacks on Ukraine in recent weeks. Dozens of people were killed by a missile and drone assault last weekend, according to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky.

Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov spoke with Secretary of State Marco Rubio over the phone on Monday to discuss the conflict.

data.title: Orlando's civility pledge should inspire us all
data.description:
Like Jews worldwide, Orlando philanthropist Alan Ginsburg was deeply shaken by Hamas’s brutal Oct. 7, 2023, attack on Israel, which killed 1,200 and took hundreds hostage. 

Soon, numerous elite U.S. university campuses erupted, displaying anti-Israel rhetoric sometimes spilling into crude antisemitic tropes. Ginsburg felt civil discussion’s decline into polarized shouting matches was unhealthy in a city of tourism and theme parks. So, he contacted the Rev. Joel Hunter, former pastor of Northland, a 20,000-member suburban megachurch.

In early 2024, Hunter and the Ginsburg Family Foundation invited 150 civic and community leaders to devise a way forward. Their Central Florida Pledge aimed to help build “America’s most welcoming community” and keep “Central Florida a safe community for all who live here and for future generations.” It read as follows:

“I will lead by example—treating all people with dignity and respect, especially those with whom I disagree. 

“I will refrain from inflammatory words and actions, and actively support those being attacked. 

“I will report threatening incidents of hate and violence to 800-423-TIPS (8477).

“I will educate myself about all forms of discrimination, including antisemitism, homophobia, Islamophobia, racism, and help others in my circle of influence to do the same.” 

So far, 6,000 people have signed, including about a thousand from area high schools and colleges.

“The pledge has made a real impact especially with young people,” said Rabbi Steven Engel, emeritus rabbi of the Congregation of Reform Judaism. A founding pledge member, Engel helped involve the interfaith community. 

Pledge members applied their credo in March 2025, after fundamentalist Christians demonstrated at several Orlando area churches, including a gay congregation and a gay-affirming United Methodist Church. Inside and outside, protesters disrupted Sunday services, calling Joy Metropolitan Ministries a “synagogue of sin.” Hunter and about 70 pledge-signers came out in support.  

In response, the Rev. Terri Steed Pierce told the Orlando Sentinel, “It meant a lot to me, but it meant even more to my congregation when they walked in to see a bunch of people they’ve never seen before standing outside saying, ‘You’re going to have to go through us to get to them,'” she said. “And ninety-nine percent of those people standing outside my gay church were not gay.”

The two pledge founders, Ginsburg and Hunter, are ideally suited for this effort.  

Ginsburg, a concert promoter turned affordable-housing developer, is a major Orlando philanthropist. His Ginsburg Family Foundation’s many causes include two Orlando hospitals, helping finance the University of Central Florida’s Hillel center, plus $6 million for a new, downtown Holocaust museum. He also co-founded and sponsors summer “Seeds of Peace” programs involving young Israelis and Palestinians. 

Hunter is hardly a typical Sunbelt preacher. A Midwesterner, the 1960s Civil Rights Movement kindled his ministry commitment. Then, in 2016, the Pulse nightclub shooting that killed 49 and wounded 58 in Orlando galvanized Hunter. 

“We knew we had gay people in the congregation,” Hunter told a reporter at the time. “But there was no one who felt like they could reach out to us, apparently. … And so, I went to the congregation and said, ‘I’m going to do a self-examination as to whether or not I have been culpable by not speaking to a broad love of all our neighbors, especially the gay community.'” 

Still, he told the Sentinel, “I’m a conservative evangelical Christian, for crying out loud. And I have not had to sacrifice one iota of what I believe” in developing the pledge. “But I have been able to figure out how I can best respect other people in what they believe.”

According to Sue Wasiolek, a faculty participant in Duke University’s Civil Discourse Project, “Efforts like the Central Florida Pledge work because they move civility from an abstract ideal into a lived, public commitment. When people see neighbors showing up across lines of difference, it reshapes what’s possible in divided communities.” 

“Born out of crisis, The Central Florida Pledge became a community-driven movement,” Ginsburg  told me in an interview. It’s “a simple step the community can take to encourage widespread safety, dignity and respect, and help make Central Florida America’s most welcoming community by neighbor.”

Durham, N.C.-based journalist and author Mark I. Pinsky covered religion for the Orlando Sentinel for 15 years.

data.title: US-Iran deal would lift 'pressure on both sides' over energy prices, Strait of Hormuz closure
data.description:
Former Defense Secretary Mark Esper on Friday said he believes a memorandum of understanding (MOU) between the U.S. and Iran – meant to get the two countries to an eventual peace deal – would lift “pressure on both sides” over the Strait of Hormuz.

“At the end of the day, all this really does is take us into a negotiating round while lifting the pressure on both sides when it comes to energy and other items moving through the Strait of Hormuz,” Esper said on NewsNation.

A tentative agreement reportedly has been reached between Washington and Tehran to extend by 60 days a fragile ceasefire in the three-month conflict, reopen the Strait of Hormuz and establish a framework for broader talks on Iran’s nuclear program.

Negotiators have been working to finalize the terms of the MOU. President Trump on Friday said he was meeting in the Situation Room “to make a final determination sign-off” on it.

In a post to Truth Social, Trump said Iran must agree that they will never have a nuclear weapon, immediately open the Strait of Hormuz with no tolls for shipping traffic, and remove all remaining mines from the waterway. He also demanded that Iran’s enriched uranium “will be unearthed by the United States” and destroyed, adding that “no money will be exchanged, until further notice.”

Iranian state media reported the deal is not yet finalized on its end either, but the regime has said any meaningful negotiations to end the war can’t begin without Tehran gaining access to $12 billion of its own money currently frozen in foreign banks.

Esper, who ran the Pentagon during Trump’s first term, said “there are some issues” with the MOU, namely that the Iranians have tried to insert language stipulating U.S. forces move out of the region, reclaiming its frozen funds, and managing the Strait of Hormuz. 

“In my view, all those things should not be included in this memorandum of understanding,” he said.

But he added that Iran “is under great pressure because they can’t sell oil on the open market,” costing them about $430 to $450 million a day. The United States and the global economy are also suffering from steep energy prices thanks to the now three-month closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a vital shipping lane through which one-fifth of the world’s crude oil and natural gas flows through in times of peace.

The closure and subsequent rising energy prices have hurt the Trump administration and frustrated Republicans as they head into the midterm elections in November.

data.title: Florida Democrat Frederica Wilson to retire from the House
data.description:
Rep. Frederica Wilson (D-Fla.), a 16-year veteran of Capitol Hill, will not seek reelection in November, the Miami Herald reported on Friday morning, becoming the 30th House Democrat from the 119th Congress who won’t be joining the 120th. 

Wilson is expected to make her formal announcement on Friday at an event in her South Florida district, where local officials are honoring her long service to the community by naming a street after her. 

Wilson, 83, had initially dismissed suggestions that this term would be her last. But she had recently missed weeks worth of votes in the Capitol, while she recovered from an eye operation, fueling speculation that she might be considering an exit. 

A long-time educator, Wilson, served as the principal of an elementary school and a member of Miami-Dade County School Board before being elected to Congress in 2010. She quickly gained prominence following the 2012 shooting death of Trayvon Martin, an unarmed Black teenager from her district who was killed by a self-appointed neighborhood watch volunteer named George Zimmerman. 

Wilson said Martin was the victim of racial profiling and called for Zimmerman’s imprisonment. (A jury acquitted Zimmerman in 2013).   

The news of Wilson’s retirement came less than a month after Florida GOP Gov. Ron DeSantis adopted a new House map for the 2026 midterms as part of the larger GOP campaign, spearheaded by President Trump, to eliminate Democratic districts in red states around the country. 

The new Florida map is expected to flip several Democratic seats to the Republicans in the midterms. But Wilson’s 24th District remains deeply blue, and she told the Herald that she had delayed her announcement to leave Congress to prevent GOP legislators from disenfranchising voters in the region. 

“With me not here, would that weaken the survival of District 24?” she said.

Shortly after the news broke, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) hailed Wilson as “an unyielding champion of our children, a defender of the disadvantaged and a voice for the voiceless.”

“For more than a decade in the United States House of Representatives,  Frederica Wilson has been a relentless advocate for our young people, fighting to increase economic growth, create jobs and lower costs for working families,” Jeffries said in a statement.

data.title: Trump says he's weighing 'final determination' on Iran deal
data.description:
President Trump said Friday he is meeting in the White House Situation Room to make a “final determination” on the tentative agreement to extend the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran and to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. 

In a lengthy Truth Social post, Trump reiterated his call for Iran to never obtain a nuclear weapon and for the vital shipping lane to immediately open without tolls or restrictions in both directions. 

Trump called for all “water mines (bombs)” placed by Iran in the strait to be “terminated” and for all enriched nuclear material to be unearthed and destroyed by the U.S. in “close coordination and conjunction” with Iran and the International Atomic Energy Agency. 

“No money will be exchanged, until further notice. Other items, of far less importance, have been agreed to,” the president wrote. 

U.S. sources confirmed Thursday that negotiators for Washington and Tehran had reached a deal on a 60-day memorandum of understanding to extend the ceasefire in the Middle East and reopen the strait. Neither Trump nor Iranian leadership has yet agreed to the proposal.

Both sides will also commit to negotiating the future of Iran’s nuclear enrichment program and U.S. sanctions on the regime, according to the agreement. 

The tentative deal also states that Iran will not impose tolls on commercial ships attempting to transit the strait, and Tehran will begin demining the waterway, according to sources. The agreement still has to be cleared by senior Iranian leadership.

If the commercial shipping in the strait is restored, the U.S. Navy blockade will be removed, Trump said.

Washington will also issue sanctions waivers, allowing Tehran to sell its oil, according to sources.

In his first comments since the potential agreement emerged, Iran’s top negotiator Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, who also serves as the speaker of the country’s parliament, wrote in a post on X that “the winner of any agreement is the one who is better prepared for war from the day after.”

data.title: Beijing's influence persists in US schools under a new name
data.description:
If students are learning Chinese in public schools, they could be using a curriculum shaped by the Chinese Communist Party. 

In 2004, Beijing began to establish Confucius Institutes on American university campuses, which in turn partnered with K-12 public schools to establish roughly 500 Confucius Classrooms. A backlash on Capitol Hill led to the closure of nearly all the Confucius Institutes, leading most to assume that the problem had been solved.

But as the National Association of Scholars documented in a 2022 report, some of the institutes survived by changing their names or transferring their operations to third-party organizations with innocuous names. This is preserving Beijing’s influence over American students.

One successor group is the Center for Bridging Cultures, a K-12 education nonprofit based in Virginia, just outside the nation’s capital. The center helps bring teachers from China into American classrooms, organizes student and administrator exchange trips to China, and continues to operate as Alfred University’s Confucius Institute’s Confucius Classroom partner with the same Chinese government and university partnerships, according to K-12 public school documents.

It has not been identified previously as a Confucius successor, but the evidence is in the public domain.

To start, there is a connection in terms of personnel. The Center for Bridging Cultures was founded in 2021, amid the mass closure of campus-based Confucius Institutes. The executive director of the center is Gao Qing, the long-time director of the Confucius Institute U.S. Center, which the State Department described as “the de facto headquarters of the Confucius Institute network.”

In August 2020, the State Department designated the headquarters as a Chinese government foreign mission, calling it “an entity advancing Beijing’s global propaganda and malign influence campaign on U.S. campuses and K-12 classrooms.”

The picture is less clear as to whether there is a funding relationship. As a tax-exempt nonprofit, the Center for Bridging Cultures does not have to publicly disclose donors, although it does have to provide that information to the IRS in an annual filing known as a Form 990. Whereas Beijing acknowledged its funding of the old Confucius Institutes, the center does not publicly identify donors.

Even so, the website of the Beijing Language and Culture University does list the Center for Bridging Cultures as one of its “global institutes” — a category that previously included many campus-based Confucius Institutes in the U.S. — and refers to it specifically as filling the roles that the Confucius Institutes once played.

Over the last two decades, the Beijing Language and Culture University had made itself into the leading sponsor of Confucius Institutes, serving as the partner for 29 of them across the globe, from the U.S. to Nepal. It also won recognition multiple times as one of the “Outstanding Confucius Institute Chinese Partners of the Year.”

According to the Beijing Language and Culture University website, the Center for Bridging Cultures has a nationwide reach and “plays a crucial role in continuing and expanding the Chinese language programs for K-12 schools and communities that were previously offered by Confucius Institutes and Confucius Classrooms.”

Beijing Language and Culture University operates directly under the supervision of the Chinese Ministry of Education, and its Confucius Institute operates in coordination with the United Front Work Department. Indeed, the university’s own organizational chart lists a Party Committee United Front Work Department as a formal administrative unit of the university.

According to the U.S. State Department, the United Front Work Department’s mission is conducting foreign influence operations designed to “neutralize sources of potential opposition.” A former head of the department even chaired the global headquarters for Confucius Institutes in Beijing.

The best sources of information about how the Center for Bridging Cultures carries out its mission are the three annual 990 forms it has filed with the IRS for 2022, 2023 and 2024. Without disclosing the identity of donors, the filings indicate the center has received more than $2.5 million in donations. Its 990 forms also show it has distributed funds across several public-school districts, private schools, a community college, a private university, an online Chinese learning platform and a Chinese immersion charter school in Washington, among others.

The center’s website advertises opportunities for American principals and district superintendents to participate in 12-day trips focused on education in China and exchange opportunities. It has also launched the U.S.-China Friendship Schools Initiative, which pairs American schools with a Chinese counterpart.

State and local education authorities could simply say no to partnering with Beijing-backed organizations, but a lack of transparency often prevents parents and lawmakers from even knowing that such partnerships exist.

That concern led to the introduction in Congress of the TRACE Act, which would make federal K-12 funding contingent on schools making information about the influence of adversaries like China available to parents. Unless amended, however, the bill would not require schools to disclose information about U.S.-based nonprofits like the Center for Bridging Cultures that remain nominally independent.

Another option for federal action would be for the IRS to conduct more thorough vetting of organizations seeking tax exempt status. Reviews could assess the relationship between applicants and entities that appear on the Defense Department’s list of Chinese military companies, the Commerce Department’s Entity List for export controls and the like.

These changes will require substantial effort. But what we cannot afford to do is sit back and let China focus the lenses through which our children are going to see the world.

Jennifer Richmond, an education adviser and China specialist, is the deputy director of Education and National Security at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies.
